item.title: Re: Missing “And” in recovery log
item.content:encoded: Looks like the error was addressed; thank you for bringing this to attention!

item.title: Missing “And” in recovery log
item.content:encoded: There is a missing “and” in the recovery log. Section reads “called to break up an arrest”. Page is fully locked so only admins can edit it. Just an FYI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! This seems to do the trick.

…Although now I wonder about upgrading these into looking like actual cards…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two years ago, scantron brought up that the divs don't work quite well on mobile. Since they still don't, I cooked up a solution Replacing the current divs with the following styling should probably fix that:

 [[div style="text-align: center; border: solid 4px #444444; width: 40%; padding: 1px 15px; margin: auto; box-shadow: 2px 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,.2);"]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Site user Permanent informed me that the Leeds police are, in actuality, the West Yorkshire Police, and I have made the change to the entry. Thank you!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Given that I was the one who added the 2236 entry, this is definitely approved and shame on me for not doing it sooner.

ETA: And added.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits have been made by the author as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so the big impact of this skip is the text featured on the cards. Someone picked up on this and included a really good test of SCP-2203 in SCP-2236's log, and giving a simple link there not only would draw users to this test, but also all the other neat tests found therein, and the crosslinks that article has to offer. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-2203 is to be kept in a standard locker and powered down between tests. Use of SCP-2203 is restricted solely for testing and not for any other purpose. Any information gathered from SCP-2203 must be logged in the experiment journal and is not to be acted upon without principal researchers' consent. All requests for hiring decisions, vacation time, or transfers to different facilities strictly on SCP-2203's advice will be denied. Researchers are expected to review the personnel guide for Foundation policies regarding ethical behavior in relationships between employees before testing.

To this:

SCP-2203 is to be kept in a standard locker and powered down between tests. Use of SCP-2203 is restricted solely for testing and not for any other purpose. Any information gathered from SCP-2203 must be logged in the experiment journal and is not to be acted upon without principal researchers' consent. All requests for hiring decisions, vacation time, or transfers to different facilities strictly on SCP-2203's advice will be denied. Researchers are expected to review the personnel guide for Foundation policies regarding ethical behavior in relationships between employees before testing.

Testing of the cards created by SCP-2203 using SCP-2236 has been approved, following review by Site Command.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I was reading this too late at night and trying to make it more complicated than it was.

+ My guess
- Hide whatever

Was it Ron Towson?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hints:

  • What's his Score?
  • Where else do we see domestic violence?
  • Why would a D-class request testing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm just being dense, but I can't figure out who the last test subject is. Hints?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I'll post that test, but it could definitely make for good tale fodder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonder what happens if Gears tries it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, he should've waited. You can't rush true love.

Of course, don't wait too long. Trust me, that window does not open twice.

Also +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me too long to work out who the last test subject was, if only because you pulled it off that subtly. Well done and a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem is that the text blocks of the cards are perfectly center-aligned. However, this wiki has a lot of white empty space on the right on mobile, for no good reason. The div is just doing what it's supposed to do, centering itself in its parent container.

On PC this empty space doesn't exist.

item.title: Re: SCPREADINGS
item.content:encoded: I know >_<

item.title: Re: SCPREADINGS
item.content:encoded: Give it a bit, it takes a while to get through her schedule. She might have SCP-2341 on her list as we speak. She also recently read SCP-2030, so she's doing more of the Series III skips now.

item.title: Re: SCPREADINGS
item.content:encoded: /me wants one of my skips narrated ;-;

item.title: Re: SCPREADINGS
item.content:encoded: Aw, yeah! I love GemoDawn's work. I'm honored. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, now you can buy the t-shirt.

Thanks, Cyantreuse!

item.title: Re: SCPREADINGS
item.content:encoded: She's good at narrating stuff.

item.title: SCPREADINGS
item.content:encoded: Fuckin' sis kids

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ending is rather ominous. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't seem to do the trick, unfortunately. I don't know enough about div blocks and phone browser development to have any chance at figuring out the answer, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think changing some of the parameters of your div blocks might fix this, you may have some conflictions going on there. Here is my recommendation:

 ~~~~~~~~ @@@@ @@@@ [[div style="text-align: center; border: solid 4px #444444; width: 280px; padding: 1px 15px; margin: auto; box-shadow: 2px 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,.2);"]] ~ TEXT ~ [[/div]] @@@@ @@@@ ~~~~~~~~

Use the @@@@ as needed, they will help space things out for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any advice how to make it work on both computer and phone platforms?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love is bourgeois It just didn't really hold my interest and the ending felt weak.

More important than my imo, however, is that those boxes are fucked up on mobile. Screenshot in a bit.

http://imgur.com/lOvdTQy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one feels a lot like a Series II SCP, due to how simple and organized it is.

Also, Series II is best series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd agree with others that the tone needs work in some spots (both the handling and the description of the rogue agent sound unprofessional), but I still quite like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon initial interview, Mr. Hamilton reported to be engaged, and refused the offer to be tested with SCP-2203. He replied that he loves his fiancée and he would rather listen to his heart than any machine.

Several parts of this don't read like something that would be in a Foundation document. Still, this is nice enough for a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test 67. Ouch. Fantastic job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's right. In fact, I said that there wasn't an anomalous compulsion to seek out the person on the card.

But that doesn't mean people don't occasionally decide to go AWOL anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if I missed it but in the description you never said that there was an anomalous compulsion for the subject to seek out the person on the card, then an agent goes AWOL just to go and find the chick on the card he was given.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awwww, this was such a nice read. I was thoroughly entertained, and it doesn't hurt that the old Shakey's Pizza we used to have in town had one of these machines just inside the entrance. I remember many an evening standing next to it, waiting in line to show my report card to the cashier to get free game tokens after my mom was done paying. The lowest rating you could get was "cold fish".

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, it's a simple and well executed idea. The testing log really brought it to life, and the formatting on the cards was a nice touch. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, the more I think about it. Testing log updated to include an initial safety log (yup, it's not going to turn you into a frog), including the reason researchers and not D-Class are being used in regular testing (a certain level of socialization is important for meaningful results, at least at the start).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You make a very valid point. I hadn't considered that.

Hmm…I may do something with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, in my first iteration it was a hole with a mismatched piece of tarp sewn over it. Now it's actually seaworthy and making sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Last night, I had this dream about you.
In this dream, I'm dancing right beside you.

Edit: Actually, this song is better suited to that one bug skip someone did for the Zyn Challenge. Still catchy, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, if we've "argued" I've already forgotten. All behind us :)

I brought it up because it seemed like such a glaring hole in the original draft; why hasn't the Foundation considered this? There was also weird language concerning it that I thought exploring asexuality/aromantic would clear up. Plus it is interesting, and the way it's presented here adds a lot of depth to the machine. It definitely knows what it's doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. I've argued a bit with Deci in the past about when it's appropriate (to the story) to include alt and non-binary sexualities into testing, and we have some differences, but this one is definitely enriched by "throwing the machine a curveball" to see what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought about that, but figure keeping the story of the item as is makes more sense. Otherwise, it starts looking like a place to declare ships. Not nearly helpful.

However, if you want to use SCP-2203 in a tale for your own characters, please, by all means, feel free.

(I also just made the cards a bit more card-like)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's Machine of Death, except with love. I'm totally okay with this.

The anomalous nature of SCP-2203 involves its ability to choose someone who reacts positively to the subject's personality, and does not impart any extra ability to strengthen the bond.

This sentence feels redundant, but other than that, I liked this, particularly the one guy who'd been picked three times already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot. Not only is it a good example of writing an excellent story within the SCP item framework, it's also a good example of how a seemingly completely beneficial anomalous item still needs containment.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I had originally planned on it, Decibelles is the one that told me to put it to the forefront. It was the right decision.

Thanks, Decibelles!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for a good anomalous love story. (Also, nice inclusion of aro/ace.)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fun idea with good execution. Might be fun to have open testing logs as an addendum for this item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the base article to be a bit bland and having a pretty basic and predictable effect, but the testing logs were nice and did add to the article. Overall, I don't know how to vote on this, so I'm going to leave it at neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love that little twist at the end with the repeat test. It did warn him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept thinking of this while reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a lot of fun to read! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I would like to mention: Considering that 2203 was found to have affected some civilians, it would be odd for the first few tests to be on the researchers and agents. I understand that you have stated that the love is not anomalous, but that would not be identified from the beginning and one may assume it is anomalous until proven otherwise.

Otherwise, the writing is alright. However, the issue I mentioned (and general not-knowing-what-all-this-is-leading-to) got me to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Not sure how that slipped by. Whatever, fixed now.

(Also fixed the "perfect match" note by noting reciprocity of results.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a nice story, have an upvote.

I think that Misaki is a feminine name, but you refer to Dr. Ohta with "his". You might want to fix that somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… An interesting idea with decent writing, but in the testing log I noticed the phrase "Testing proves that a perfect match is possible". That just doesn't feel like wording that belongs in a scientific document (is there a way to objectively measure a "perfect match"?)

Other than that small gripe, it seems like a rather decent article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this…I like this a lot, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because we're all taught that we only need to find our special someone to live happily ever after.

Photo under Creative Commons license on Flickr by frankieleon.
